coredata.dc:title: Coupling urchin-like TiO2 nanospheres with nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots for visible-light-induced degradation of toluene
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Juntao
Zhu, Jiaxin
Liu, Luzhen
Xia, Lin
He, Zhiqiao
Wang, Da
Xu, Xing
Song, Shuang
coredata.dc:description: The development of efficient catalysts for the photocatalytic degradation of volatile organic compounds is paramount. Herein, urchin-like TiO2 nanospheres were coupled with nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (NSGDs) through a facile impregnation method. The resulting NSGDs/TiO2 composite forms an S-scheme heterojunction, owing to work function difference, which facilitates the effective separation of photogenerated charge carriers. The NSGDs/TiO2 exhibits an impressive 99.8 % toluene conversion efficiency and 50 % mineralization efficiency during 6 h of visible light illumination. Furthermore, this composite retains its catalytic activity over five consecutive cycles. The remarkable performance is attributed to the augmented visible light absorption and reduced recombination of photogenerated carriers, stemming from the increased electron density of Ti and the establishment of an internal electric field. Photogenerated holes, superoxide radicals, and hydroxyl radicals are identified as the key active species in this process.

HR-TEM images and corresponding IFFT images (insets) of (a) bare TiO2, (b) GDs/TiO2, (c) NGDs/TiO2, and (d) NSGDs/TiO2; (e) elemental mapping images for NSGDs/TiO2.

(a) XPS full survey of NSGDs/TiO2 and high-resolution scanning XPS spectra of (b) C 1 s, (c) Ti 2p, and (d) O 1 s region for bare TiO2, GDs/TiO2, NGDs/TiO2, and NSGDs/TiO2; (e) N 1 s region for NGDs/TiO2 and NSGDs/TiO2, and (f) S 2p region for NSGDs/TiO2.

(a) UV–vis DRS and (b) Tauc plot of NSGDs/TiO2, NGDs/TiO2, GDs/TiO2 and bare TiO2, (c) VB-XPS spectra of NSGDs/TiO2, NGDs/TiO2, GDs/TiO2 and bare TiO2, (d) PL spectra of NSGDs/TiO2 and bare TiO2 ; (e) transient photocurrent traces and (f) EIS spectra recorded for NSGDs/TiO2, NGDs/TiO2, GDs/TiO2 and bare TiO2.
(a) Photocatalytic degradation of toluene and (b) the CO2 generation on the catalysts.

(a-b) gas phase quenching experiments in different atmosphere, (c-d) scavenging experiments using K2Cr2O7, PBQ, TEMPO as corresponding ecb -, •O2 – and •OH scavenger; EPR spectra of NSGDs and bare TiO2 using DMPO (e) in water for •OH and (f) in ethanol for •O2 –.

head.ce:title: Coupling urchin-like TiO2 nanospheres with nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots for visible-light-induced degradation of toluene

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of efficient catalysts for the photocatalytic degradation of volatile organic compounds is paramount. Herein, urchin-like TiO2 nanospheres were coupled with nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (NSGDs) through a facile impregnation method. The resulting NSGDs/TiO2 composite forms an S-scheme heterojunction, owing to work function difference, which facilitates the effective separation of photogenerated charge carriers. The NSGDs/TiO2 exhibits an impressive 99.8 % toluene conversion efficiency and 50 % mineralization efficiency during 6 h of visible light illumination. Furthermore, this composite retains its catalytic activity over five consecutive cycles. The remarkable performance is attributed to the augmented visible light absorption and reduced recombination of photogenerated carriers, stemming from the increased electron density of Ti and the establishment of an internal electric field. Photogenerated holes, superoxide radicals, and hydroxyl radicals are identified as the key active species in this process.

ce:keyword.ce:text: N and S co-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Toluene is a dominant volatile organic compound (VOC) commonly emitted in processes where it is used as a solvent or an essential raw material in the production of organic chemicals [1,2]. Various control strategies such as biological treatment, adsorption, thermal oxidation, absorption, and non-thermal plasma have been extensively applied for toluene abatement [3,4]. Photocatalytic oxidation, known for its facile operation, pollution-free nature, and low energy consumption, is recognized as a promising approach for air purification [5]. TiO2 is an attractive photocatalyst due to its photoactivity, high chemical stability, non-toxicity, cost-effectiveness, and environmental friendliness [6,7]. However, the major limitations of TiO₂ include its poor photoenergy utilization and the rapid recombination of photoinduced charge carriers, which largely restrict its widespread application in toluene treatment [8].

The tailoring of TiO2 nano- or micro-architectures with unique morphologies enables enhanced photoenergy utilization. TiO2 can be classified as zero-, one-, two-, or three-dimensional materials [9–13]. Zero-dimensional TiO2 nano- or microspheres are known for their superior light-harvesting capabilities because a sufficient amount of light can access the material interior [10]. One-dimensional fibers or tubes have been shown to reduce the recombination of photogenerated carriers and to increase the transfer rate of interfacial charge carriers, benefiting from their high surface-to-volume ratios. [11]. Two-dimensional nanosheets offer improved photo-to-chemical energy conversion, owing to their tiny thickness and large surface area for light exposure [12]. Three-dimensional interconnected structures contribute to heighten carrier mobility and increase light-harvesting efficiency via multiple scattering within their porous framework [13]. As a consequence, controlling the structural dimensions of TiO2 is advantageous for maximizing its desirable properties.

The three-dimensional urchin-like mesoporous TiO2 hollow spheres feature the excellent characteristics of large specific surface area, remarkable light-scattering effect, and direct charge transport channels, which collectively enhance the light absorption and the transportation of photogenerated charge carriers [14]. Nevertheless, the inherent drawback of the wide bandgap (3.2 eV) in anatase TiO2 hinders its effectiveness in the visible light region [15]. Extensive research has been conducted to extend the light response of TiO2 to the visible-light region, which includes strategies such as photosensitization, heteroatom doping, charge transfer interactions, composites with other semiconductors, noble metal deposition, modification with rare-earth elements, and the incorporation of ionic liquids during the synthesis [16,17]. Recently, coupling TiO2 with graphene quantum dots (GDs) has emerged as a promising strategy for activating its photoactivity under visible light [18]. The large surface area and quantum confinement effect of GDs confer unique structural, optical, and electronic properties to the material. For instance, GDs maintain or even surpass the performance of two-dimensional graphene and carbon quantum dots in the photocatalytic process [18]. The ultra-small GDs exhibit a higher number of oxygen-containing functional groups that serve as highly active reaction sites for the photocatalytic reaction, compared to two-dimensional graphene materials [18]. Moreover, the band gap of GDs can be easily adjusted because of their distinct quantum confinement and edge effects [19]. Furthermore, the conductivity of GDs is higher than that of carbon quantum dots, attributed to their graphene lattice and superior crystallinity [20].

For the purpose of maximizing the desired performance of GDs in photocatalysis, nitrogen and sulfur co-doping emerges as an effective strategy to modify their physicochemical properties. The nitrogen and sulfur co-doped GDs (NSGDs) had a superior extinction coefficient over the visible spectrum with a higher Fermi level, enabling them to absorb more photon energy for exciting valence band electrons [21].

Because of the respective advantages of urchin-like TiO2 nanospheres and NSGDs, urchin-like TiO2 nanospheres decorated with NSGDs (NSGDs/TiO2) are promising to simultaneously extend visible-light response and promote photogenerated-carrier separation. Despite their potential, the application of such visible-light-responsive photocatalysts for VOCs abatement remains unexplored. Hence, in this study, novel NSGD-decorated urchin-like TiO2 nanospheres were prepared by a straightforward procedure and applied for the visible-light-induced catalytic degradation of toluene. A batch reactor with an intermittent feeding mode was employed for toluene decomposition due to its inherent advantage of better process control and optimization, well-defined residence time, and higher reaction efficiency as well as the flexibility for easy scale-up [22]. The performance, stability and universality of NSGDs/TiO2 were systematically assessed. Furthermore, the reactive oxygen species (ROS) and toluene degradation pathways were studied, and the photocatalytic mechanism of NSGDs/TiO2 for toluene degradation under visible light was also proposed.
The used reagents are displayed in Text S1. All the reagents were of analytical grade and used as received.
Urchin-like TiO2 nanospheres were synthesized using a targeted etching strategy [14]. The detailed synthetic method is described in Text S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GDs, NGDs and NSGDs
ce:section.ce:para: The solution of undoped GDs was synthesized using a hydrothermal method. First, 5.04 g of citric acid monohydrate and 1.44 g of NaOH were dissolved in 120 mL water and stirred for 6 h until a clear solution was formed. Subsequently, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave with a volume of 200 mL and kept at 180℃ for 6 h. Next, ethanol was added to the above hydrothermal solution that was then subjected to centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. The final GDs solution was obtained by collecting the supernatant separated from the solution.

Similarly, a solution of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGDs) was prepared using the nitrogen-source urea instead of NaOH. Furthermore, to synthesize the NSGDs solution, in addition to the citric acid monohydrate, thiourea—as the sulfur and nitrogen source—was used. All these solutions were stored in dark conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GDs/TiO2, NGDs/TiO2 and NSGDs/TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: The GDs/TiO2 composites were prepared via an impregnation method. Typically, 90 mL of the GDs solution was mixed with 0.3 g of TiO2 powder in a 100 mL beaker, followed by continuous stirring for 24 h in the dark. After being washed with water and collected through suction filtration, the obtained powder was vacuum dried for 24 h at 80℃. NGDs/TiO2 and NSGDs/TiO2 composites were also synthesized by the same procedure. The graphene quantum dot content in the as-prepared composites was calculated by the mass of GDs bonded divided by the mass of TiO2 added, where the mass of GDs bonded was the mass change of TiO2 sample after impregnation process. The practical weight percentages of graphene quantum dots in GDs/TiO2, NGDs/TiO2 and NSGDs/TiO2 were finally determined to be 4.81 wt%, 4.95 wt%, and 4.97 wt%, respectively. Moreover, the reference NSGDs/Al2O3 composite, was synthesized using Al2O3 powder as the substrate.

The physical structures were investigated using X-ray diffraction (XRD), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS), photoluminescence (PL) spectra, Brunauer − Emmett − Teller (BET) surface areas, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR). Details are presented in Text S3.
Additional details of photoelectrochemical measurements, including transient photocurrent responses, electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS), and Mott–Schottky plot, are available in Text S4.

The photocatalytic degradation of toluene was performed in a sealed stainless-steel reactor equipped with a 6.5-cm-diameter optical window. The reactor had a volume of 200 mL. A 300 W xenon lamp (Beijing Electric Light Sources Research Institute, China) was utilized as the visible light source, in conjunction with a 400 nm cutoff filter (the UV–vis transmittance of the cutoff filter was displayed in Fig. S1). The visible light source was positioned approximately 20 cm above the reactor. The light intensity at the top of the reactor was measured as 100 mW/cm2 using a visible light radiometer (FZ-A, Beijing Normal University, China). A water bath system was employed to maintain a constant temperature throughout the reaction process, and a magnetic stirrer was positioned at the bottom of the reactor to ensure efficient circulation of the reactant gas during the reaction process.

Typically, 100 mg of the photocatalyst was uniformly dispersed onto a round glass plate (3.5 cm in diameter) that was placed at the center of the reactor. Subsequently, the reactor was sealed and purged with high-purity air to remove impurities. The gas mixture containing toluene and water vapor was then injected into the reactor such that the initial toluene concentration was 1000 ppm and the relative humidity was 50 %. Prior to the photocatalytic oxidation, the reactor was magnetically agitated and kept under dark conditions for 90 min to realize the adsorption–desorption equilibrium on the catalyst surfaces. Next, the 300 W xenon lamp was turned on, and the concentration of toluene withdrawn at preset intervals while the reaction was being carried out and analyzed using a gas chromatograph (Agilent 6890 N, Agilent, USA) with a flame ionization detector. Further, the CO2 yield was monitored using a gas chromatograph (Agilent 7890B, Agilent, USA) equipped with a thermal conductivity detector.
The conversion efficiency of the toluene and mineralization rates were obtained from Eqs. (1) and (2), respectively.
Conversion efficiency = (1-Ct /C 0) × 100 % (1).
Mineralization efficiency = (C CO2/(7 × C 0)) × 100 % (2).
where C 0 and C are the concentrations of toluene before and after 6 h of irradiation, respectively, and C CO2 is the concentration of CO2 at the end of the reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gas-phase capture and radical scavenging experiments
ce:section.ce:para: The conditions of the gas-phase capture experiment are similar to those of the photocatalytic degradation experiment, except that air is replaced with N2 and the relative humidity (RH) is reduced to nearly zero. The free-radical quenching experiments were conducted by adding 0.01 g of the active-species scavenger with K2Cr2O7 as the electron scavenger, TEMPO as the hydroxyl-radical (•OH) scavenger, and PBQ as the superoxide-radical (•O2 −) scavenger [23].

The phase compositions of the as-synthesized samples were characterized using XRD. As depicted in Fig. S2, the anatase TiO2 structure (JCPDS card No. 04–0477) predominates in all samples. The presence of GDs shows a negligible impact on the crystal structure of TiO2 nanospheres. Additionally, the diffraction peak assigned to GDs is barely detectable, indicating a low GDs concentration on the TiO2.

The surface morphologies of different samples, including bare TiO2, GDs/TiO2, NGDs/TiO2, and NSGDs/TiO2, were observed using FE-SEM and HR-TEM techniques. As depicted in Fig. 1 , all samples exhibit spherical particles with radially oriented rod-like structures on them. The diameter of TiO2 nanospheres ranges between 500 and 700 nm, and the rods measure approximately 40–70 nm in length. The decoration of GDs does not significantly alter the overall morphology of the TiO2 particles. Nonetheless, HR-TEM images in Fig. 2 b–d reveal the presence of numerous nanodots (2–5 nm) on the TiO2 surface in the GDs/TiO2, NGDs/TiO2, and NSGDs/TiO2 samples. The top right insets in Fig. 2b–d display the inverse fast Fourier transformation (IFFT) images of these dots. The lattice spacing distance of 0.24 nm corresponds to the graphite (1120) facets, indicating that the carbon atoms in GDs, NGDs, and NSGDs possess a sp2-bonded graphitic structure [24]. The IFFT images at the bottom right of Fig. 2a–d show clear lattice fringes with a d-spacing of 0.35 nm, representing the (101) plane of anatase TiO2 [25]. These observations confirm the successful synthesis of NSGDs/TiO2 hybrids. The elemental mapping analysis of NSGDs/TiO2, as shown in Fig. 2e, indicates a homogeneous distribution of Ti, C, N, O, and S elements, implying that the NSGDs are highly dispersed on the surfaces of the urchin-like TiO2 nanospheres.

N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of various catalysts (Fig. S3a and Table S1) exhibit typical type IV isotherms with H3 hysteresis loops, indicative of mesoporous structures in all samples. The specific surface areas of bare TiO2, GDs/TiO2, NGDs/TiO2, and NSGDs/TiO2 are measured to be 103.2, 108.6, 102.9, and 100.5 m2/g, respectively. It can be seen that GDs incorporation exerts a minimal effect on the intrinsic pore structure of urchin-like bare TiO2. Compared with GDs/TiO2, the slightly decreased surface areas of NGDs/TiO2 and NSGDs/TiO2 are probably due to the restacking of doped GDs caused by the free electrons of incorporating heteroatom [26,27]. The pore size distribution curves in Fig. S3b reveal that the pore diameters of all catalysts are mainly concentrated in the range of 10 to 15 nm, further verifying their mesoporous nature. Such mesoporous structure of the urchin-like materials not only allows for the exposure of more active sites but also provides efficient transport pathways for easier diffusion of guest molecules to access the inner active sites, contributing to the enhanced photocatalytic activity [14,28]. Moreover, micropores are also known to be highly reactive sites for adsorption and catalysis. However, no micropore surface area are detected for the three GD-decorated TiO2 samples as compared with bare TiO2 (Table S1), which may be attributed to the GDs deposition that blocked the micropores of urchin-like TiO2. Thus, the porosity of GD-decorated TiO2 comprises mainly mesoporosity.

Raman spectroscopy is a pivotal tool capable of characterizing the structural properties of graphitic materials and semiconductors. The Raman spectra of pristine GDs, NGDs and NSGDs samples (Fig. S4a) feature two prominent characteristic peaks of graphitic carbon at 1443 cm−1 (D band) and 1581 cm−1 (G band) [29], which demonstrated that single N doping and N and S co-doping did not destroy the graphitic structure of GDs. Notably, the D and G bands observed in the heteroatom-doped GDs deviate from the typical D and G bands associated with carbon materials, exhibiting distinct ranges (D ∼ 1200–1300 cm−1, G ∼ 1400–1500 cm−1). It is generally recognized that the D band is related to the disordered nature originating from the structural defects, small size effect and dangling sp2 carbon bonds, whereas the G band is associated with the E 2g vibrational mode in-plane for sp2 hybridized carbon atoms and reflects the crystalline nature of a carbon material [30]. The red-shift of the D and G bands for the heteroatom-doped GDs could be attributed to the reduction in size and the S and N doping that break the symmetry of carbon atoms and increase the disordered structure [31]. As for the Raman spectrum of bara TiO2 (Fig. S4b), four strong peaks were clearly visible at about 147, 399, 520, and 640 cm−1, respectively assigned to the E g(1), B 1g, A 1g, and E g(2) modes of anatase TiO2 phase [32]. In the case of the GDs/TiO2, NGDs/TiO2, and NSGDs/TiO2 composites, besides the four peaks mentioned above, two additional peaks associated with the graphitic structure of GDs were also observed, which confirmed the successful formation of various GDs with TiO2. Furthermore, compared with bare TiO2, a significant blue shift of the E g(1) vibration mode of anatase TiO2 were observed for the composites, indicating a robust interaction between TiO2 and various GDs.

The elemental compositions and chemical states of various samples were investigated using XPS. As depicted in Fig. 3 a, NSGDs/TiO2 primarily consists of C, N, O, S, and Ti elements. The exact atomic percentage of C, N, O, S, and Ti elements are 21.06 at.%, 2.58 at.%, 52.19 at.%, 0.23 at.%, and 22.94 at.%, respectively. The atomic percentage of the detected elements in the GDs/TiO2 and NGDs/TiO2 are also presented in Table S2. It is clear that all the three GD-decorated TiO2 samples possess the similar C atomic content and C/Ti atomic ratio, indicating their similar loading amounts of GDs on the TiO2 surface. The deconvoluted C 1 s spectrum of NSGDs/TiO2 (Fig. 3b) can be resolved into three peaks. The principal peak at 284.5 eV corresponds to the sp2-hybridized carbon in the graphene framework [33], while the two peaks located at 285.85 eV and 288.08 eV are attributed to the sp3-hybridized carbon of C–O, C–N, C–S, and C = O of carbonyls and carboxylates, respectively [34]. No peak is observed at around 282 eV, indicating the absence of Ti − C bond [35]. The Ti 2p spectra (Fig. 3c) display two peaks at approximately 458.40 and 464.10 eV, respectively, corresponding to the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 states of TiO2 [36,37]. The spin-orbital doublet splitting of 5.7 eV confirms the Ti4+ oxidation state in TiO2 [38]. Notably, a downward shift in the binding energy of Ti 2p is observed in the different GD-decorated TiO2 samples, indicating an increased electron density on Ti atoms due to the strong interaction between GDs and TiO2 [39]. The core-level spectrum of O 1 s (Fig. 3d) reveals peaks for Ti–O–Ti (∼529.6 eV) [40], Ti–OH (531.5 eV) [41], and H–O–H bonds of chemisorbed H2O (532.6 eV) [42]. Unlike the bare TiO2 spectra, the peak at 532.6 eV disappeared in the three GD-decorated TiO2, while a new peak corresponding to Ti–O–C appeared at ∼ 531.2 eV [43], suggesting the formation of the GD-decorated TiO2 hybrid possibly through the Ti–O–C bonds.

In contrast to the GDs/TiO2 spectra, the N 1 s spectrum of NGDs/TiO2 and NSGDs/TiO2 (Fig. 3e) exhibits three peaks at 399.4, 400.2, and 401.4 eV, which are assigned to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N in NGDs and NSGDs, respectively [44]. No signal assignable to the Ti–N bond (∼396 eV) is observed in the spectra, which indicates that the nitrogen atoms are not doped into the TiO2 lattice [35]. For the S 2p XPS spectra of NSGDs/TiO2 (Fig. 3f), three characteristic peaks can be deconvoluted at 163.1, 164.3, and 168.3 eV, which are assigned to the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 of thiophene structure and S = O bonding, respectively [45]. However, the peak assigned to the Ti–S bond cannot be detected at 162.1 eV, implying that sulfur species did not substitute lattice O atoms in TiO2 [46]. The C 1 s, N 1 s, and S 2p XPS results collectively confirm the successful doping of N and S heteroatoms into the GDs structure.

UV–vis DRS was performed to evaluate the light absorption properties of bare TiO2 and various GD-modified TiO2 samples. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a, GDs modification has a negligible influence on the optical absorption of TiO2. However, NGDs/TiO2 and NSGDs/TiO2 exhibit an obvious red shift in the absorption edge. The UV absorption edges for bare TiO2, GDs/TiO2, NGDs/TiO2, and NSGDs/TiO2 are identified at 397, 401, 427, and 442 nm, respectively, corresponding to bandgaps of 3.12, 3.09, 2.90, and 2.80 eV, respectively, as calculated using the Kubelka–Munk function and Tauc plot (Fig. 4b). Compared with the bare TiO2, the decrease in the bandgap of various GD-modified TiO2 hybrids is because of the electronic coupling between the π states of GDs and the conduction band (CB) states of TiO2, resulting in the creation of new energy levels in the TiO2 bandgap and thereby lowering the bandgap energy [17,47]. In addition, due to the electron-donor nature of the nitrogen atoms, doping N into GDs provides extra electron to the π system across the C atoms, which would weaken the integrity of the π-conjugated system and induce the hybridization of frontier orbitals between N atoms and GDs [48] Such orbital hybridization would introduce additional energy levels between the π and π*, thus reducing the bandgap energy of GDs and improving the optical absorption property [48,49]. Similarly, co-doping of S and N could further lower the bandgap of NGDs by inducing more structural distortion and larger orbital hybridization, because of the larger radii and extra electrons of the S atoms as compared to N atoms [48]. As a result, the NSGDs/TiO2 exhibits a narrower energy bandgap and broader absorption than those of the NGDs/TiO2 and GDs/TiO2. The phenomenon also agrees with those of previous studies in which the introduction of quantum dots narrowed the bandgap of TiO2 [39,50]. In addition, GD-modified TiO2 samples demonstrate substantial background absorption in the visible-light region, indicating the presence of GDs on the TiO2 surface.

The valance band XPS (VB-XPS) spectra were measured to evaluate the extra electronic states above the valence band of various catalysts, and the results are shown in Fig. 4c. The VB edge energy is determined by the intersection point of the extrapolated tangent with the baseline. Compared to bare TiO2, which exhibits a VB edge energy of 2.67 eV, the VB edge energy for GDs/TiO2 slightly shifted toward the vacuum level, which should be attributed to the Ti–O–C interaction. In addition, the VB edge energies of GDs/TiO2, NGDs/TiO2, and NSGDs/TiO2 are found to be similar, which indicates that nitrogen and sulfur species are not incorporated into the TiO2 crystal lattice.

To investigate the photoluminescence properties of pristine GDs and doped-GDs that correlated to the activity, PL spectra of pristine GDs, NGDs and NSGDs were recorded at 340 nm excitation wavelength. As depicted in Fig. S5, the pristine GDs exhibited a broad peak broad emission peak at 440 nm, while the PL emission peaks of NGDs and NSGDs were centered at 530 nm and 536 nm, respectively. The redshifts for the emission peaks of doped-GDs were attributed to their reduced bandgaps, because of the introduction of intermediate levels between π and π* orbitals by these electron-donating elements (N and S) [51]. Moreover, the PL intensity of NSGDs at the same concentrations is much enhanced as compared to that of NGDs and GDs, which indicated that more delocalized electrons were present in NSGDs. Generally, the GDs in the CDs based composites can serve as photosensitizers and effective conductive mediums for electron transfer [52]. To investigate the role of NSGDs in the NSGDs/TiO2 during the photocatalytic process, PL spectra of bare TiO2 and NSGDs/TiO2 were also collected. As illustrated in Fig. 4d, the emission peak centered around 415 nm for bare TiO2 was ascribed to the self-trapped excitons localized on TiO6 octahedra [53], while the broad peak for NSGDs/TiO2 around 533 nm mainly originated from the NSGDs emission. It is obvious that the PL intensity of TiO2 decreases after coupling with NSGDs. The partial quenching of PL intensity in the NSGDs/TiO2 spectrum implies that the presence of NSGDs efficiently lengthen the lifetime of photogenerated carriers in TiO2. To further evaluate the role of NSGDs in suppressing the photogenerated carrier recombination, the transient photocurrent response was assessed.

Fig. 4e shows the transient photocurrent response at an applied potential of 1.23 V vs. reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) under chopped light illumination with a light/dark cycle of 50 s. Upon switching the light on and off, a rapid rise and fall in photocurrent were observed for all the photocatalysts, indicating efficient charge transport within the semiconductors. The NSGDs/TiO2 achieved the maximal photocurrent density of 3.89 μA cm−2, which is ∼ 8-fold higher than that of bare TiO2. The photocurrent density variation in different GD-decorated TiO2 samples correlates with their toluene photodegradation performance: NSGDs/TiO2 > NGDs/TiO2 > GDs/TiO2 > bare TiO2. The results demonstrate that NSGDs significantly enhance the generation and transport of photoexcited electrons.

To evaluate the effect of the GDs on the charge-carrier-migration capability of TiO2, EIS measurements of various samples were conducted under dark conditions at open circuit voltage. All the Nyquist plots comprise semicircular and linear segments, representing the charge transfer resistance and diffusion between electrolyte and electrode, respectively. The smaller circle diameter in the plot indicates higher electronic conductivity of the samples. As shown in Fig. 4f, the Nyquist plot diameter of NGDs/TiO2 is smaller than that of GDs/TiO2 and bare TiO2, confirming reduced charge transport resistance in NGDs/TiO2 compared to GDs/TiO2 and bare TiO2 in non-photoexcited state. This enhancement is attributed to the introduction of pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic nitrogen functional groups in nitrogen-doped GDs. Studies have shown that the high electronic conductivity of GDs is due to the delocalized π bonds caused by the sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in their intrinsic graphitic structure [54,55]. Doping with heteroatom disrupts the ideal sp2 hybridization of carbon atoms in GDs, which would create charge sites and lead to the redistribution of spin and charge densities, improving the electronic conductivity [56,57]. Here, three types of N were incorporated into the NGDs. Among these N types, pyridinic N and pyrrolic N could donate one or two electrons, respectively, to the π system of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms as delocalized electrons [58]. Therefore, it could be inferred that the pyrrolic N plays the major role in reducing the charge transport resistance. Furthermore, NSGDs/TiO2 exhibits a smaller Nyquist plot diameter than NGDs/TiO2, indicating that the addition of sulfur atoms can further improve the charge transport capacity of NGDs [54,57]. It is well reported that the N and S co-doping has a simultaneous effect on the enhancement of the asymmetrical spin and charge density in GDs [56,57,59]. Introduction of S atoms into the NGDs can further modulate its electronic structure to increase the charge density of those C atoms adjacent to N dopants, which makes the structure more easily polarized than the NGDs and thereby significantly enhancing electrical conductivity [50,56].

The photocatalytic activity of the prepared photocatalysts was evaluated by performing the degradation of gaseous toluene under visible-light illumination. After the adsorption equilibrium was reached, the photocatalytic degradation of toluene was conducted to assess the photocatalytic performance of bare TiO2, GDs/TiO2, NGDs/TiO2, and NSGDs/TiO2. For comparison, commercial P25 TiO2 and NSGDs/Al2O3 were also tested under similar conditions. As seen in Fig. 5 a, merely 5.1 % of toluene was removed without any catalyst, indicating that direct photolysis by visible light irradiation had a minimal effect on toluene degradation. In addition, negligible toluene reduction was observed under dark-condition adsorption even after 360 min of illumination with NSGD/Al2O3, revealing the limited photocatalytic ability of NSGDs. The bare TiO2 achieved an 80 % toluene removing efficiency at the end of the reaction. In contrast, all GD-decorated TiO2 samples exhibited enhanced photocatalytic activities. These results suggest that the incorporation of GDs into TiO2 significantly boosts the photodegradation and removal of toluene. Among different GDs/TiO2 samples, the NSGDs/TiO2 sample showed the highest toluene removal efficiency, reaching 99.8 % after 360 min, which was nearly 1.3-fold, 1.2-fold, and 1.1-fold higher than that of bare TiO2, GDs/TiO2, and NGDs/TiO2, respectively. As revealed in the UV–vis DRS, EIS, and transient photocurrent response measurements, incorporating single N-doped GDs and N, S co-doped GDs narrow the band gap of TiO2, extending the spectral response of hybrids to visible light region. The introduction of heteroatom doped GDs also mediates the electronic properties of TiO2, hindering the recombination of photogenerated carrier separation in TiO2 and leading to enhanced photocatalytic activity. Noteworthily, owing to the presence of discrete pairs of electrons on sulfur atoms, the S-doping could induce the enhanced asymmetrical spin and charge density on NGDs structure and cause the simultaneous cooperative effect of N and S [60,61], which further indirectly improves the visible light absorption capability, charge conductivity, and photogenerated carrier separation of NGDs/TiO2. This is the reason why the NSGDs/TiO2 hybrid exhibits better photocatalytic performance in comparison to NGDs/TiO2.

Mineralization efficiency is a crucial parameter for assessing photocatalytic effectiveness, as it often involves multi-step reactions including the oxidation of toluene and the photocatalytic conversion of by-products to CO2 and water. Fig. 5b demonstrates that the CO2 yield followed the following trend: NSGDs/TiO2 > NGDs/TiO2 > GDs/TiO2 > P25 > bare TiO2. For NSGDs/TiO2, the highest CO2 yield and mineralization efficiency reached 3518 ppm and 50 %, respectively, after 360 min of irradiation. To exclude the possibility that the CO2 come from the spillage of carbon in NSGDs/TiO2, NGDs/TiO2, GDs/TiO2, or bare TiO2, the control experiments were carried out with catalyst only (no toluene) under the irradiation. As depicted in Fig. S6, the CO2 yields from the photolysis of all catalysts were found to be 219 ppm, which are all close to the atmospheric background level of CO2 (381 ppm). This result confirmed that the generated CO2 was only from toluene mineralization. Moreover, it should be noted that despite P25 exhibited higher toluene removal efficiency than GDs/TiO2 and NGDs/TiO2, its mineralization efficiency was lower, which was likely due to surface deactivation of P25 caused by the accumulation of stable intermediates [62]. Furthermore, compared with recently reported photocatalysts (Table 1 ), the NSGDs/TiO2 also displays a superior performance to other modified TiO2, which evidences that the integration of NSGDs with TiO2 is advantageous for the visible-light degradation of toluene.

To identify the primary active species in NSGDs/TiO2 during the photocatalytic process, gas-phase capture and free-radical quenching experiments were carried out. When nitrogen gas replaced air in the photocatalytic system, almost no toluene reduction was observed, demonstrating the vital role of the electron pathway in the visible-light-induced radical process (Fig. 6 a–b). As an electron acceptor, molecular oxygen captured photogenerated electrons (ecb -) to form •O2 – radicals (Eq. (3)), which were primarily responsible for toluene degradation. Furthermore, altering the relative humidity from 50 % to nearly zero had negligible impact on toluene removal efficiency. This finding suggests that toluene itself could act as an electron donor, undergoing direct oxidation by photogenerated holes. Under low humidity conditions, toluene is susceptible to attack by holes, leading to the formation of benzyl radicals [63]. However, the mineralization efficiency substantially decreased from 50 % to 23.3 %, indicating that the oxidation of toluene by •OH radicals based on holes (hvb +) pathway could hardly be ignored in the presence of H2O (Eq. (4)).
(3) ecb - + O2 → •O2 –
(4) hvb + + H2O → H2O+ → H+ + •OH

For the free-radical quenching experiments (Fig. 6c-d), the degradation of toluene was obviously inhibited upon the addition of TEMPO (known as an •OH scavenger) [23], indicating that the •OH radical was the primary active species responsible for toluene degradation. Moreover, quenching experiment using PBQ as an •O2 – scavenger revealed a significant inhibition in toluene degradation [23], confirming the existence of •O2 –. Similarly, when K2Cr2O7 was added as an effective electron scavenger in the reaction system [6], the toluene degradation was also significantly impeded. Together with the results of N2 control and PBQ quenching experiments, this finding further indicated that •O2 –, arisen from the electron pathway (Eq. (5)), play a crucial role in the oxidative degradation of toluene.

To further ascertain the reactive species generated by NSGDs/TiO2 in the degradation of toluene, EPR tests were conducted using DMPO as a spin trap reagent under visible light irradiation (wavelength > 400 nm) [68]. As depicted in Fig. 6(e-f), the intensities of DMPO–•O2 – and DMPO–•OH signals in NSGDs/TiO2 were almost negligible in the dark, whereas these intensities significantly increased under light conditions, indicating the generation of •O2 – and •OH radicals in the photocatalytic degradation of toluene. Therefore, •O2 – and •OH radicals are identified as the dominant ROSs during the photocatalytic degradation process. This observation further validates the findings from the gas-phase capture and scavenging experiments.

The catalytic performance of a photocatalyst depends markedly on its structural, optical and electronic properties. As revealed in the XRD, FE-SEM, HR-TEM, and BET measurements, coupling TiO2 with NSGDs had negligible influences on its crystal structure, morphology and specific surface area, which implies that the enhanced photocatalytic performance of TiO2 by the surface-decorated NSGDs was mainly caused by other factors. The successful coupling of NSGDs on TiO2 was confirmed by the Raman characterization. Simultaneously, XPS analysis further verified that the TiO2 was coupled with NSGDs through the Ti–O–C bonds. From UV − vis DRS, the band gap of TiO2 was narrowed by the incorporation of NSGDs and the light response of TiO2/NSGDs was extended to visible light region, due to the electronic coupling between the π states of NSGDs and the CB states of TiO2 [17,47]. This means more charge carriers will be generated on the TiO2/NSGDs under visible light illumination. Moreover, TiO2/NSGDs demonstrates a lower intrinsic charge transfer resistance than bare TiO2 in EIS measurement, ascribed to the high electrical conductivity and charge mobility of NSGDs [54,56]. The separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs on the TiO2/NSGDs was also significantly enhanced compared with that on bare TiO2, confirmed by transient photocurrent response measurement. This affirmation indicates that more charge carriers photogenerated on the TiO2/NSGDs could participate in the degradation reaction. Therefore, it can be concluded that the activity improvement of TiO2/NSGDs hybrid mainly derives from the increased amounts of charge carriers that caused by rapid and efficient separation.

To further explore the internal charge transfer pathway between TiO2 and NSGDs, their band structures were investigated. The actual E VB and E CB potentials of NSGDs were ascertained through electrochemical characterization via linear potential scan [51]. As indicated in Fig. 7 a, the cathodic and anodic scanning results give E CB and E VB values of about − 0.52 V and 1.76 V (vs. normal hydrogen electrode (NHE)), respectively. The band-gap energy (E g) of NSGDs is then calculated to be 2.28 eV. The Mott–Schottky plot (Fig. 7b) established the flat-band potential (E FB) of n-type bare TiO2 as − 0.24 V. The conduction band edge for an n-type semiconductor typically lies about 0.1 V more than the E FB [69]. Integrating these findings with the Tauc plot results, the E CB and E VB of bare TiO2 were determined to be − 0.34 V and + 2.78 V, respectively.

Based on the determined band positions, the transfer of photogenerated charge carriers between TiO2 and NSGDs may follow either the type-II or S-scheme heterostructure mechanism. According to the type-II heterojunction mechanism (as depicted in Fig. 8 a), the photogenerated electrons on the CB of NSGDs should migrate to the CB of TiO2, whereas the photogenerated holes are accumulated on the VB of NSGDs. However, the E VB of NSGDs (+1.76 V vs. NHE) is lower than the oxidation potential of H2O to •OH (E θ = +2.38 V vs. NHE) [70], which would impede the production of •OH radicals. This analysis, based on the redox potential, contrasts with the EPR analysis and quenching experiments, which indicate that •OH radicals are the predominant ROSs. Thus, the conventional type-II heterojunction mechanism is not suitable here to explain the charge transfer in NSGDs/TiO2. An alternative scenario is the formation of an S-scheme heterojunction between NSGDs and TiO2 upon contact (Fig. 8b). Studies on electron trapping capabilities report that the work functions of anatase TiO2 and NSGDs are approximately 7.18 eV and 4.71 eV [71–73], respectively. Consequently, TiO2 has a lower Fermi level (E f) than NSGDs before they contact. When anatase TiO2 is coupled with NSGDs, the electrons in NSGDs will prefer to transfer to TiO2, as evidenced by the XPS spectra (Fig. c-d) showing a downward shift in the binding energies of Ti 2p and O 1 s. Such electron transfer leads to a downward bending of the band edge of TiO2 due to electron accumulation and an upward bending of the band edge of NSGDs owing to electron depletion until an equilibrium Fermi level (E f) is reached [72,74], which culminate in the creation of an internal electric field at NSGDs/TiO2 interfaces pointing from NSGDs to TiO2. Under visible light irradiation, the VB electrons of both TiO2 and NSGDs are excited to their respective CB. The interfacial internal electric field and bent bands drive the transfer of photogenerated electrons from the CB of TiO2 to combine with the holes in the VB of NSGDs, thereby constructing the S-scheme heterojunction between TiO2 and NSGDs.

To further elucidate the charge transfer route in NSGDs/TiO2 during the photocatalytic process, in-situ XPS spectra were measured under visible light illumination. As illustrated in Fig. 9 , the binding energies of Ti 2p and O 1 s in NSGDs/TiO2 under light irradiation shift to higher energy levels, whereas the binding energies of C 1 s, N 1 s, and S 1 s move to lower energy levels. These shifts in binding energy unequivocally indicate that the photoexcited electrons migrate from the TiO2 to NSGDs under illumination. This migration pattern across the NSGDs/TiO2 interface accords with the S-scheme charge transfer route, corroborating the hypothesized heterojunction structure.

In addition, as mentioned above, •O2 – radicals that formed by the reaction of molecular oxygen with photogenerated electrons (Eq. (3)) were primarily responsible for toluene degradation. Previous density function theory (DFT) studies have confirmed that N and S co-doping changed the asymmetrical spin and charge density of carbon atoms of GDs. The calculated adsorption energies of O2 on the C atom in GDs as well as N atom and S atom in NSGDs were −0.36, −0.83 and −1.00 eV, respectively [60,61,73]. This affirmation indicates that NSGDs is more favorable for O2 adsorption, which is conducive to the formation of •O2 – radicals.

From the presented results and discussions, a plausible mechanism for the photocatalytic degradation of toluene by the NSGDs/TiO2 hybrid is proposed. Upon light irradiation, the hybrid absorbs photons to generate excitons. The light-scattering effect caused by the three-dimensional urchin-like hollow morphology of TiO2 and the reduction of bandgap originating from NSGDs incorporating promote the visible light absorption to generate more photo-excitons. Under the action of interfacial internal electric field and bent bands, the photogenerated electrons are spontaneously transferred from the CB of TiO2 to the VB of NSGDs and combined with the hole in NSGDs VB. This S-scheme charge transfer mode efficiently separates photogenerated electron-hole pairs, simultaneously maintaining the high electron reducibility in the NSGDs CB and the hole oxidizability in the TiO2 VB. Additionally, the mesoporous structure inherent in NSGDs/TiO2 not only exposes more active sites but also promotes the mass transfer of reactants from the gas phase to the surface. Subsequently, the photogenerated electrons in the CB of NSGDs activate oxygen molecules to form •O2 –, while the photogenerated holes in the VB of TiO2 either directly oxidize adsorbed toluene or interact with water molecules to yield •OH. These generated ROSs and holes serve as the primary oxidants and ultimately oxidize toluene into CO2, H2O and other intermediates.

The reusability of NSGDs/TiO2 was evaluated for its potential in practical applications. Toluene photodegradation tests were performed to evaluate the photostability of NSGDs/TiO2. The toluene removal efficiency with NSGDs/TiO2 remained above 97 % across five successive cycles (Fig. S7). In addition, no appreciable changes were observed in the phase composition, chemical states, and elemental compositions of NSGDs/TiO2 before and after the tests, as confirmed by XRD, FTIR, and XPS results (Fig. S8). These results demonstrate that NSGDs/TiO2 has robust stability for photocatalytic degradation of toluene.

To better illustrate the reusability of NSGDs/TiO2 for photocatalytic degradation of toluene, the maximum number of times that the catalyst can be reused was studied. Notably, the NSGDs/TiO2 could be reused at least ten times and the toluene removal efficiency was still maintained above 91.7 % (Fig. S7). However, the toluene removal underwent a significant decline to 77.2 % after the twelfth cycle. As confirmed by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) analysis (Text S5 and Fig. S9), the slight deactivation of the reused NSGDs/TiO2 could be ascribed to the accumulation of degraded intermediates (i.e., benzoic acid, benzyl alcohol, and benzaldehyde) on the surface of catalyst [7]. Moreover, it should be remarked that the photocatalytic activity of the used NSGDs/TiO2 was recovered to its original level after regeneration by irradiation treatment (details were described in Text S6), indicating the possibility of long-term use of the catalyst.

The detection of intermediates and their toxicity prediction during the toluene degradation process is an environmental issue that needs to be considered. Gas chromatograph-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) method was used to analyze the possible intermediates of toluene degradation, and five small molecules with open-chain structures were identified as the main degradation intermediates in the gas phase (Table S3). Combined with the results of aforementioned HPLC analysis and published literatures [62,63], two possible transformation pathways of toluene were proposed as illustrated in Fig. 10 a. The first pathway of toluene decomposition is the hydrogen abstraction of the methyl group on the benzene ring accompanying with the addition of •OH to form benzyl alcohol (P1). The alcohol group in P1 can be further oxidized by •OH, leading to the formation of benzaldehyde (P2) and benzoic acid (P3). The second pathway is the direct hole oxidation of toluene to form benzyl radical, which then reacts with molecular oxygen to produce P2. Subsequently, these intermediates containing benzene rings undergo a ring-opening reaction to yield low-molecular organics such as 2-(2-butoxyethoxy)-ethanol (P4), 2,3-dimethyl-2-Butene (P5), 1-propoxy-2-butene (P6), butanal (P7), and acetone (P8). With the continuous attack of ROSs, all of the intermediates would be finally mineralized to CO2 and H2O.

The potential toxicity of toluene and its degradation intermediates was predicted by using the Toxicity Estimation Software Tool (T.E.S.T.). Fig. 10b-d illustrate the predictive values of oral rat LD50, development toxicity, and mutagenicity of toluene and its eight intermediates. As shown in Fig. 10b, the LD50 values of P4, P5, P6, and P8 were higher than that of toluene, meaning the reduced acute toxicity of these intermediates. However, some intermediates, including P1, P2, P3, and P7, experienced increased toxicity, particularly P1 was ranked as a “moderate toxic” compound. It was noteworthy that P1, P2, and P3 were the main intermediates adsorbed on the surface of catalyst, which would not increase the toxicity of gases. From Fig. 10c, except for the P6 and P7, the toluene and other intermediates were “Developmental non-toxicants”. In addition, Fig. 10d revealed that all degradation intermediates were categorized as “Mutagenicity negative”, highlighting low carcinogenic and genetic toxicity risks. Therefore, the comprehensive results indicate that the residual toxicities of toluene and its degradation intermediates were diminished after the photocatalytic oxidation process.

To investigate the universal applicability of NSGDs/TiO2, another two typical VOCs (i.e., benzene and 1,2-dichlorobenzene) were further chosen to examine their degradation in the NSGDs/TiO2 photocatalytic system. The experimental conditions for photocatalytic degradation align with those used for toluene. As shown in Fig. S10, the degradation efficiencies of benzene and 1,2-dichlorobenzene both reached almost 100 % after six hours of visible light irradiation, and their mineralization efficiencies were 56 % and 63.5 %, respectively. These results testify that the NSGDs/TiO2 possesses satisfactory universal applicability for the photocatalytic degradation of various VOCs.

In conclusion, this study has demonstrated that a hybrid photocatalyst composed of NSGDs and urchin-like TiO2 nanospheres significantly enhances the photocatalytic degradation of gaseous toluene under visible light irradiation. The NSGDs/TiO2 catalyst achieved 99.8 % toluene degradation and 50 % mineralization within 360 min of visible light irradiation. The simultaneous effect of increased electron density around Ti atoms and the resulting internal electric field significantly improves the light energy absorption and the separation of photogenerated carriers. In addition, the NSGDs/TiO2 exhibited satisfactory reusability, stability, and universal applicability for various VOCs. This hybrid presents a promising alternative catalyst for the photocatalytic degradation of gas-phase toluene.
